walkthrough.title: Choose Your Wonder Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Seven Wonders set up. First, shuffle the seven wonder cards and deal one to each player, or let everyone choose a board by agreement. Each wonder board has two sides, A and B. Side A is simpler and recommended for your first game. Place your wonder board in front of you. This is your city for the game.

walkthrough.title: Note Your Starting Resource
walkthrough.text: Look at your wonder board. It shows one starting resource in the upper left corner. This resource is produced by your city every single turn for free. It is permanent and never gets used up. Different wonders start with different resources, so check what yours produces. You will need this resource and others to build structures.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Coin Bank
walkthrough.text: Place all the coin tokens in a shared bank in the center of the table. There are value-one coins and value-three coins. Players can make change between them at any time. Each player takes three value-one coins from the bank as their starting money. Keep your coins visible near your wonder board.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Conflict Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place all the conflict tokens in a shared supply near the bank. These are the military victory and defeat tokens. You will not need them until the end of each age, when military conflicts are resolved. There are tokens worth plus one, plus three, and plus five for victories, and minus one tokens for defeats.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Age Decks
walkthrough.text: Now let's build the three age decks. This is the trickiest part of setup. Each age card has a small number showing the minimum player count it is used for. For example, a card marked three-plus is used in games with three or more players. Remove all cards that exceed your player count. If you have five players, remove all cards marked six-plus and seven-plus.

walkthrough.title: Handle the Guild Cards
walkthrough.text: The Age Three deck has special purple guild cards. You do not use all of them. Count your players and add two. That is how many guild cards you include. So with five players, keep seven guild cards. Randomly select that many from the full set, and shuffle them into the Age Three deck. Return the unused guilds to the box.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Each Age Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Age One deck thoroughly and set it face down. Do the same for Age Two and Age Three. Keep them in separate stacks, clearly labeled or ordered. You will deal from Age One first, then Age Two, then Age Three. Each age has exactly six turns.

walkthrough.title: Seat Your Neighbors
walkthrough.text: In Seven Wonders, the players sitting directly to your left and right are your neighbors. You can only buy resources from your neighbors, and you only compare military strength with your neighbors. Make sure everyone knows who their left and right neighbors are. This adjacency is central to the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Age One Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal seven cards from the Age One deck to each player. Pick up your cards and look at them secretly. This is your starting hand for the first age. You will choose one card to play each turn, then pass the rest to your neighbor. Everyone plays simultaneously, so there is no turn order to worry about.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. Each player has a wonder board with a starting resource, three coins, and seven Age One cards in hand. The coin bank and conflict tokens are in the center. The Age Two and Age Three decks are set aside for later. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Structure
walkthrough.text: Seven Wonders is played over three ages. Each age has six turns. On every turn, all players choose and resolve their actions simultaneously. After six turns, the age ends and military conflicts are resolved. Then you deal a new hand of seven cards from the next age deck and continue.

walkthrough.title: The Simultaneous Turn
walkthrough.text: On each turn, everyone picks one card from their hand and places it face down. Once all players have chosen, everyone reveals their card at the same time. Each player resolves their chosen action, then passes their remaining hand to a neighbor. In Age One, pass left. In Age Two, pass right. In Age Three, pass left again.

walkthrough.title: Your Three Action Choices
walkthrough.text: When you reveal your card, you do one of three things with it. First, you can build the structure by paying its cost and adding it to your city. Second, you can build a stage of your wonder by tucking the card face down under your wonder board and paying the wonder stage cost. Third, you can discard the card for three coins from the bank. You must do exactly one of these three things.

walkthrough.title: Building a Structure
walkthrough.text: To build a structure, check its cost in the upper left corner. Some structures are free. Others cost resources, coins, or both. You pay the cost using resources your city produces. Your city produces resources from your wonder board, your brown cards, your gray cards, and some yellow cards. Resources are not consumed when used. You produce the same resources every turn.

walkthrough.title: Buying Resources from Neighbors
walkthrough.text: If you cannot produce all the resources a card requires, you may buy the missing ones from your neighbors. Each missing resource costs two coins, paid to the neighbor who produces it. You can only buy from the players immediately to your left and right. You can buy from their wonder board, brown cards, and gray cards, but not from certain yellow or wonder resources that are owner-only. Your neighbors cannot refuse a sale.

walkthrough.title: Free Chain Building
walkthrough.text: Some Age Two and Age Three cards show a prerequisite structure name in their cost area. If you already have that prerequisite structure in your city, you can build the new card for free, skipping its normal cost entirely. This is called chaining. Always check for chains before paying resources, because a free build saves you coins and resources.

walkthrough.title: The Duplicate Rule
walkthrough.text: You cannot build two structures with the same name in your city. If you already have a Marketplace, you cannot build another Marketplace. Before committing to a build, check that you do not already have a copy. You can still use a duplicate card for a wonder stage or discard it for coins.

walkthrough.title: Building Wonder Stages
walkthrough.text: To build a wonder stage, take the card you chose and tuck it face down under your wonder board. The card's identity does not matter. It is just a construction marker. Pay the cost printed on the wonder stage, not the card's cost. Wonder stages must be built from left to right, in order. Each stage provides a benefit shown on the wonder board.

walkthrough.title: Discarding for Coins
walkthrough.text: If you do not want to build a structure or a wonder stage, you can discard your chosen card and take three coins from the bank. This is useful when your hand has nothing you need or can afford. However, on the sixth and final turn of each age, you receive only two cards. You play one normally and discard the other with no coin reward.

walkthrough.title: Card Colors and Types
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the seven card colors. Brown cards produce raw materials like wood, stone, ore, and clay. Gray cards produce manufactured goods like glass, loom, and papyrus. Blue cards give direct victory points. Green cards are science cards that score via a special formula. Yellow cards provide commerce benefits, discounts, and sometimes coins or resources. Red cards add military shields. Purple guild cards appear only in Age Three and score based on conditions.

walkthrough.title: Passing Hands
walkthrough.text: After everyone resolves their action, pass your remaining cards to your neighbor. In Age One, pass left. In Age Two, pass right. In Age Three, pass left. This is the drafting mechanic. What you pass affects what your neighbor can play next turn. Sometimes taking a card you do not need to deny it to a neighbor is the right play.

walkthrough.title: The Sixth Turn
walkthrough.text: On the sixth turn of each age, you receive a hand of only two cards. Choose one to play normally, building a structure, building a wonder stage, or discarding for coins. The other card is simply discarded face down. You do not receive three coins for this discarded card. It is just removed from the game.

walkthrough.title: Military Conflict Resolution
walkthrough.text: At the end of each age, compare your military strength with each of your two neighbors separately. Count the shields on all your red cards. If you have more shields than your neighbor, you win that conflict and take a victory token. If you have fewer, you take a minus-one defeat token. If tied, nothing happens. Victory tokens are worth plus one in Age One, plus three in Age Two, and plus five in Age Three.

walkthrough.title: Science Scoring
walkthrough.text: Green science cards show one of three symbols: a gear, a compass, or a tablet. Science scoring uses two methods that add together. For identical symbols, score the square of the count. So two gears score four points, three gears score nine, and four score sixteen. For complete sets, each set of all three different symbols scores seven bonus points. Both methods apply simultaneously, so you might score nine from identical gears plus seven from a complete set.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After Age Three military conflicts, add up your final score in seven categories. Military conflict tokens, net positive or negative. Coins, worth one point per three coins rounded down. Blue civilian card points. Green science points. Yellow commerce card points, if any grant VP. Purple guild points. And wonder stage points. Add everything together. Highest total wins. If tied, the player with the most coins wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Age
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Seven Wonders! We will walk through a scripted first age with four players. Each player has a different wonder board with a unique starting resource. Everyone has three coins and a hand of seven Age One cards. All actions happen simultaneously, so let's take them one turn at a time.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your First Hand
walkthrough.text: Look at your seven cards. You will see different colors representing different types: brown for raw materials, gray for manufactured goods, blue for victory points, green for science, yellow for commerce, and red for military. For your first few plays, prioritize brown and gray resource cards. These are the foundation that lets you build everything else later.

walkthrough.title: Turn One: Everyone Builds
walkthrough.text: Let's say Player 1 plays a Lumber Yard, which produces wood for free. Player 2 plays a Stone Pit, producing stone. Player 3 plays a Loom, giving them cloth production. Player 4 looks at their hand and spots a Tavern, a yellow card that gives five coins for free. All four players reveal simultaneously and resolve their builds.

walkthrough.title: Why Resources Come First
walkthrough.text: Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3 all chose resource cards. This is smart because Age One is about building your production base. Without resources, you cannot afford the more powerful cards in Ages Two and Three. Player 4 took five coins, which is useful but does not expand their production. Let's see how that plays out.

walkthrough.title: Pass Your Hand Left
walkthrough.text: Now everyone passes their remaining six cards to the left. Pick up the new hand you received. Notice how the cards you did not take are now available to your neighbor. This is the drafting mechanic. What you pass matters as much as what you keep.

walkthrough.title: Turn Two: Expanding Production
walkthrough.text: Player 1 builds a Clay Pool for free, adding clay production. Player 2 builds a Glassworks for glass. Player 3 spots a Scriptorium, a green science card showing a tablet symbol. It costs one papyrus to build, and Player 3 does not produce papyrus. But their right neighbor does, so Player 3 buys papyrus for two coins. Player 4 builds a Barracks, a red military card.

walkthrough.title: Buying Resources in Action
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what Player 3 just did. They needed papyrus to build the Scriptorium but did not produce it. Their neighbor had a gray card producing papyrus, so Player 3 paid two coins to that neighbor. The neighbor's production is never consumed. The neighbor still produces papyrus on future turns. This commerce system lets you build cards even when you lack the resources personally.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Ignoring Resources
walkthrough.text: Player 4 now has five coins and one military card, but zero resource production beyond their wonder's starting resource. They look at the passed hand and see a great blue card worth three victory points, but it costs two resources they cannot produce. Player 4 would need to buy both from neighbors, costing four coins. That is almost their entire treasury for one card.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Economy Before Points
walkthrough.text: Building your resource base in Age One is essential. Without production, every card costs you coins for neighbor purchases, and you will run out of money fast. Player 4 skipped resources for coins and military, but now they cannot afford the structures they want. The lesson: invest in brown and gray production early so you can build freely in Ages Two and Three.

walkthrough.title: Turn Three: A Chain Opportunity
walkthrough.text: Player 1 notices a card in their hand with a chain symbol. It is an Apothecary from Age One that will chain into a future Age Two card for free. Player 1 builds the Apothecary to set up the chain. Smart long-term thinking. Player 2 builds another brown resource card. Player 3 adds a second science card. Player 4 finally builds a brown resource card.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Building a Wonder Too Early
walkthrough.text: On the next turn, Player 4 decides to build a wonder stage, tucking a card under their board. But the wonder stage costs two stone and one wood. Player 4 produces only stone from their wonder board. They buy wood and a second stone from neighbors for four coins. They are now almost broke. Meanwhile, the hand they received has two strong cards they cannot afford to build.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Time Your Wonder Stages
walkthrough.text: Building wonder stages is important, but timing matters. Use a wonder stage to get value from a weak hand, not when you have good cards to build. If the hand you received has strong structures, build those instead and save the wonder stage for a turn when the hand is mediocre. Also, make sure you have the resources or coins to afford the stage without going broke.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Ignoring Military
walkthrough.text: Actually, let's rewind. Player 4 built one red card early but then abandoned military. Their left neighbor has been steadily building shields. At the end of Age One, Player 4 has one shield while their left neighbor has three. Player 4 loses that comparison and takes a minus-one token. Those negative tokens add up over three ages.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Your Neighbors' Military
walkthrough.text: You do not need to win every military conflict, but you should try to at least tie your neighbors. Repeated minus-one tokens across three ages add up to minus three or worse. A single red card costs the same as any other structure but prevents a defeat token that effectively adds two points of swing. Keep an eye on your neighbors' shield counts and match them when possible.

walkthrough.title: End of Age One: Military Resolution
walkthrough.text: After all six turns, it is time for military conflict. Each player compares their total shields with their left neighbor and their right neighbor separately. Player 1 has two shields and beats their left neighbor's one, earning a plus-one token. Player 3 has zero shields and loses to both neighbors, taking two minus-one tokens. Every player resolves both comparisons.

walkthrough.title: Dealing Age Two
walkthrough.text: Shuffle and deal seven cards from the Age Two deck. Notice that Age Two cards are more powerful but also more expensive. This is where your resource investments from Age One pay off. Pass direction changes in Age Two — you will pass right instead of left. That means your other neighbor now receives your passed cards.

walkthrough.title: Free Chain in Action
walkthrough.text: Player 1 looks at their Age Two hand and spots a Dispensary that chains for free from the Apothecary they built in Age One. While other players pay resources and coins for their builds, Player 1 plays the Dispensary for absolutely nothing. That is the power of setting up chains early. It is like getting a free turn.

walkthrough.title: Continue Through Ages Two and Three
walkthrough.text: Play continues through all six turns of Age Two, with military resolution at the end. Then deal Age Three, which includes the powerful purple guild cards. Guild cards score based on conditions like counting your neighbors' defeat tokens or brown cards. The game ends after Age Three's military resolution.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up all seven scoring categories: military tokens, coins divided by three, blue card points, green science points, yellow card points, purple guild points, and wonder stage points. The highest total wins. In our practice game, Player 1's chain strategy and balanced building gave them a strong finish. Player 4 learned valuable lessons about resource timing.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have played through your first game of Seven Wonders! You saw how resource production, chain building, military awareness, and wonder timing all work together. Player 4 learned three key lessons: build your economy before chasing points, time wonder stages for weak hands, and keep an eye on neighbors' military.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build Your Civilization
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on resource planning, science scoring, and military pacing.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card colors, science formulas, and wonder stages.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Build Your Civilization
bullets: Check out General Tips → resource planning, science scoring, military pacing
Reference the Appendix → card colors, science formulas, wonder stages
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Resource Planning
walkthrough.text: Your resource base determines what you can build for the rest of the game. In Age One, aim for two to three production cards. Diversify your resources when possible, because Age Two and Three cards often need multiple types. Check what your wonder board already produces and fill gaps. Also look at what your neighbors produce, because you can buy from them. Building resources that your neighbors lack is especially valuable because they may need to buy from you.

walkthrough.title: Science Strategy
walkthrough.text: Science scoring has the highest ceiling in Seven Wonders but requires commitment. The scoring formula rewards collecting identical sets of symbols (squared values) and complete sets of all three symbols (seven points each). Going for science halfway is usually worse than not going at all. If you start science, commit to it. Three of one symbol scores nine points. Add a complete set and that is sixteen. Four of one symbol scores twenty-five. The payoff is enormous if you can collect enough.

walkthrough.title: Military Pacing
walkthrough.text: You do not need to dominate military. You just need to beat your neighbors. Each age, check how many shields your left and right neighbors have and aim to tie or exceed them. Winning all three ages against one neighbor earns you nine points total, while losing all three costs only three. That is a twelve-point swing for just a few red cards. One or two military cards per age usually keeps you competitive.

walkthrough.title: Commerce Card Value
walkthrough.text: Yellow commerce cards are often underrated. Cards like the Trading Post and Marketplace reduce the cost of buying from neighbors from two coins to one coin per resource. This effectively doubles your purchasing power for the rest of the game. Other yellow cards provide resources that only you can use, meaning neighbors cannot buy them. These let you build expensive cards without sharing.

walkthrough.title: Denial Drafting
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best play is not the card that helps you the most, but the card that hurts your neighbor. If you see a card in the passed hand that would give your neighbor a huge science set or complete a military sweep, take it even if you just discard it for three coins. This is called denial drafting or hate drafting. It is especially important in Age Three when guilds can score eight to ten points for the right player.

walkthrough.title: Wonder Stage Timing
walkthrough.text: Build wonder stages when your hand is weak. If the seven cards you receive have nothing useful for your city, tuck one under your wonder and get the stage benefit. Do not waste a strong hand on a wonder stage. Additionally, some wonder stages give you resources or coin income that is more valuable early in the game. Others give pure victory points that are equally valuable whenever you build them.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Neighbors
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what your neighbors are building. If your left neighbor is heavily invested in military, you either need to keep up or accept the defeat tokens. If your right neighbor has lots of blue civilian cards, they are scoring points steadily. Knowing your neighbors' strategies helps you make better drafting decisions and informs when to switch between offense and defense.

walkthrough.title: Score Distribution
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players in Seven Wonders do not put all their eggs in one basket. They collect points from multiple categories. A typical winning score might be eight from military, six from coins, twelve from blue cards, nine from science, and six from wonder stages. Pure science builds can win big, but most consistent wins come from balanced scoring across four or five categories.

bullets: Aim for 2-3 production cards in Age I

Fill gaps your wonder doesn't cover; exploit neighbor gaps

bullets: Highest ceiling but requires commitment — halfway = bad

Complete set of 3 symbols → 7 bonus; both methods stack

summary.title: Military Pacing

1-2 red cards per age usually sufficient

summary.title: Reading Your Neighbors

Know their strategy to make better drafting decisions
Neighbor awareness informs offense vs defense choices

summary.title: Score Distribution
bullets: Balanced scoring across 4-5 categories wins most games
Pure science can win big but is inconsistent
Typical win: 8 military + 6 coins + 12 blue + 9 science + 6 wonder

walkthrough.title: Science Commitment Strategy
walkthrough.text: Full science commitment is the highest-variance, highest-ceiling strategy. The key decision is whether to go all in on one symbol or diversify across all three. Four of one symbol scores sixteen from identical plus contributes to sets. Three of each symbol scores twenty-seven from identical plus twenty-one from sets, totaling forty-eight points from science alone. If you commit to science, prioritize gray resource production early because most green cards need manufactured goods.

walkthrough.title: Military Pressure Strategy
walkthrough.text: Instead of passive military pacing, aggressive military aims to win every conflict against both neighbors. Winning all six comparisons across three ages earns eighteen victory points just from military. This strategy works best when your neighbors are ignoring red cards. Build one to two military cards per age, timed to be just above your neighbors' shield count. The threat of military spending also forces neighbors to waste picks on red cards they would rather not take.

walkthrough.title: Commerce Engine Building
walkthrough.text: A commerce-focused strategy uses yellow cards to reduce neighbor purchase costs and generate coins. With the Trading Post, Marketplace, or similar discount cards, buying from neighbors costs only one coin per resource instead of two. This lets you build expensive Age Two and Three cards without investing heavily in your own production. The coins you save become victory points at the end, and some yellow cards directly score points.

walkthrough.title: Blue Card Value Scoring
walkthrough.text: Blue civilian cards offer guaranteed, predictable victory points with no special conditions. A strategy focused on blue cards aims to draft high-value blue structures throughout all three ages. Age Three blue cards can be worth seven or eight points each. This approach is reliable and hard for opponents to counter, since blue points cannot be reduced. Pair blue cards with just enough military to avoid defeat tokens.

walkthrough.title: Chain Optimization
walkthrough.text: Experienced players plan their chains before the game starts. Know which Age One cards chain into which Age Two cards, and which Age Two cards chain into Age Three. A well-planned chain path gives you two to three free builds over the game. That is effectively two to three extra turns where you save all your resources and coins. Prioritize chain starters in Age One even over slightly better standalone cards.

walkthrough.title: Neighbor Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Advanced play means reading your neighbors' production and exploiting it. If your left neighbor has strong brown card production, you can skip building brown cards entirely and buy from them instead. Use the resources you save building production to instead build scoring cards. Combine this with a Trading Post or Marketplace to reduce the cost to one coin per resource. Your neighbor becomes your resource engine.

walkthrough.title: Guild Card Selection
walkthrough.text: Purple guild cards in Age Three are the most variable-scoring cards in the game. Some guilds score based on your neighbors' cards, others on your own. Before Age Three begins, assess which guilds would score highest given the board state. If your neighbors have many defeat tokens, the Strategists' Guild scores big. If they have many brown cards, the Workers' Guild pays off. Prioritize guilds that align with the existing game state.

walkthrough.title: Adaptive Strategy
walkthrough.text: The best Seven Wonders players do not lock into a strategy during setup. They read the first two to three hands of Age One and adapt. If science cards are flowing freely because no one else wants them, pivot to science. If your neighbors are ignoring military, grab a couple of red cards for easy wins. If the hands are full of blue cards, lean into civilian scoring. The draft is dynamic, and the best strategy is the one that exploits what the table gives you.

walkthrough.title: Wonder Board Synergies
walkthrough.text: Each wonder board has unique stage rewards that favor certain strategies. Boards that grant science symbols support green card strategies. Boards with military shields reduce red card needs. Boards that give free builds or extra turns create unique opportunities. Before the game starts, assess your wonder's strengths and plan a strategy that leverages its stages. Side B boards are more complex but often more powerful than Side A.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final turns of Age Three, every decision is about maximizing points per action. Calculate the point value of each option: building a structure, completing a wonder stage, or discarding for coins. Sometimes discarding for three coins is worth one point from the coin conversion. A seven-point blue card is obviously better. But building a wonder stage that grants five points and a bonus effect might be better still. Always compare your options numerically in the endgame.

summary.title: Science Commitment Strategy
bullets: Full science = highest ceiling (3 of each = 48 points)
4 identical = 16 from sets alone
Requires gray production early — most greens need manufactured goods

summary.title: Military Pressure Strategy
bullets: Win all 6 comparisons across 3 ages = 18 VP from military
Works best when neighbors ignore red cards
Threat forces neighbors to waste picks on military

summary.title: Commerce Engine Building
bullets: Yellow discount cards: neighbor purchases 2→1 coin
Skip heavy own production → buy from neighbors cheaply
Saved coins = VP at endgame (1 VP per 3 coins)

summary.title: Blue Card Value Scoring
bullets: Guaranteed VP — no conditions, can't be countered
Age III blue cards worth 7-8 VP each
Pair with minimal military to avoid defeat tokens

summary.title: Chain Optimization
bullets: Plan chains before game: Age I → Age II → Age III free builds
2-3 free builds = 2-3 extra effective turns
Chain starters in Age I worth more than better standalone cards

summary.title: Neighbor Exploitation
bullets: Skip own production → buy from neighbor's brown/gray cards
Discount cards reduce buy cost to 1 coin
Neighbor becomes your resource engine

summary.title: Guild Card Selection
bullets: Guilds score based on game state — assess before Age III
Strategists' Guild → neighbor defeat tokens; Workers' Guild → neighbor brown cards
Draft guilds aligned with existing board state

summary.title: Adaptive Strategy
bullets: Don't lock strategy at setup — read the draft
Science flowing freely? Pivot to green
Neighbors ignoring military? Grab easy red wins

summary.title: Wonder Board Synergies
bullets: Boards with science symbols → support green strategy
Boards with shields → reduce red card needs
Side B boards more complex but often more powerful

summary.title: Endgame Point Maximization
bullets: Compare point value of every option numerically
3 coins discard = ~1 VP; 7-point blue = 7 VP
Always calculate best VP per action in final turns

entries.definition: Your personal city board showing a starting resource and wonder stages to build. Each board has two sides: A (simpler) and B (more complex/powerful). Seven unique wonders available.
The resource printed on the upper-left of your wonder board. Produced every turn for free, never consumed. Functions like a permanent brown or gray card.
Raw material production cards. Produce wood, stone, ore, and/or clay. Resources are persistent and never consumed when used. Available in all three ages.
Manufactured goods production cards. Produce glass, loom (cloth), and/or papyrus. Resources are persistent. Essential for science and many Age II/III cards.
Civilian structures that provide direct, guaranteed victory points. Values increase across ages. No conditions or dependencies — pure scoring.
Science structures showing one of three symbols (gear, compass, tablet). Score via identical-symbol squares and complete-set bonuses. High ceiling when committed.
Commerce structures that provide coins, resource discounts for neighbor purchases, or owner-only resources. Some grant VP at end of game. Essential for economic flexibility.
Military structures that add shield icons to your city. Shields compared with neighbors at end of each age. More shields = victory tokens.
Special Age III cards that score based on conditions (neighbor cards, own cards, etc.). Number used equals player count plus two. Randomly selected each game.
The players sitting directly to your left and right. You can only buy resources from neighbors and compare military only with neighbors.
Pay 2 coins per resource to a neighbor who produces it. Buyable sources: wonder starting resource, brown cards, gray cards. Cannot buy owner-only yellow or wonder resources. Neighbor cannot refuse.
Some Age II/III cards can be built for free if you already own the prerequisite card from a previous age. The chain symbol and prerequisite name are shown on the card.
Built by tucking any card under your wonder board and paying the stage's printed cost. Must be built left to right. Card identity does not matter — it is just a marker.
Score the square of the count of each identical symbol. One = 1 point, two = 4, three = 9, four = 16. Applied to each of the three symbols separately.
Each complete set of all three different symbols (gear + compass + tablet) scores 7 bonus points. Stacks with identical-set scoring.
At end of each age, compare shields with left and right neighbor separately. Win = +1 (Age I), +3 (Age II), +5 (Age III). Lose = -1 defeat token. Tie = nothing.
At game end, score 1 VP per 3 coins, rounded down. Coins accumulated from discards, commerce cards, and wonder stages.
Cannot build two structures with the same name in your city. Duplicate cards can be used for wonder stages or discarded for coins.
On the sixth turn of each age, receive 2 cards. Play 1 normally, discard the other face down with no coin reward.
Age I: pass left. Age II: pass right. Age III: pass left. Direction determines which neighbor receives your passed cards.
Before each game, remove cards above current player count. Cards marked with 3+, 4+, etc. Example: 5-player game uses 3+, 4+, 5+ cards only.
Taking a card specifically to prevent a neighbor from getting it, even if you discard it for 3 coins. Especially valuable for high-value science or guild cards.
Resources are never consumed when used. Your production is available every turn regardless of what you build. Bought resources are valid only for the current turn.
Coins earned this turn cannot be spent for same-turn purchases. Budget using turn-start coins only.
Seven categories: military tokens, coins (1 VP per 3), blue civilian VP, green science, yellow commerce VP, purple guild VP, wonder stage VP. Highest total wins; tiebreak = most coins.
